text:
God-Image and Zombie Category in a Post-Modernity Different from Pre-Modernity and Mod-ernity Within the framework of traditional, static God-image(s), the transition from pre-modernity to mod-ernity, and then to a post-modernity different from both, introduces a tension that challenges the classical divine ministry to become a new divine ministry of compassion. As humans journey through the social processes of change and transformation, many God images are reduced to zombie category status because the human situation to which they addressed religious understanding has long since changed. This article argues, first, that the ancient Hellenistic and Roman world view categories, which have traditionally underpinned the biblical concept of God as an all-powerful *Pantokrator*—a power that causes whatever it wishes—also qualify as zombie categories.

Life is a Black Swan and thus needs a radical rethink from ‘omni-categories’ to what the author calls ‘pathos-categories’ in a theopaschitic world view. The word ‘pathos’ refers to feeling, often gut feeling or empathy, so “pathos-categories” describes categories of divine feeling or emotion towards humans as the Creator takes a sympathetic involvement in our lives in general and in our suffering in particular. Describing God’s power in the terms of ‘ta splanchna’ (bowel-compassion) or in terms of ‘vulnerable faithfulness’ the author prefers to see God as being what he terms a “Sustainable Companion” involved and in touch with human suffering. This involves passing from the “omniscience” model of God to what the author terms “infiniscience” which means being involved, an infinite involvement being God’s verb or action-word rather than the being or God the noun. Rather than there being any ‘theology of the cross’ in any historical sense in the terms first expressed by Feuerbach or Barth, what is needed is a present theology of the cross that in order to radically re-evaluate the cross needs to dispense with God portrayed as an Emperor sitting upon a throne from whom even divine power is divorced from any form of compassionate empathy and needs instead to embody that kind of “divine” in the world of human experience via ‘a theology of the intestines’ whereby we return to a kenotic Christ who embodies justice whilst allowing God also to be revealed through a sympathetic fellow-suffering in a world where life is essentially the zigzag Black Swan activity and phenomenon which we embody as human beings.
explanation: The text shows a coherent, theory-driven scholarly style with specialized theological terms, which is common in human authors. The unusual coinages and odd hyphenation patterns are minor anomalies that could hint at AI generation or heavy editing, though they do not prove it.
